--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-Source:setPitch* *love-Source:setPitch*
|Source:setPitch|

Sets the pitch of the Source.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setPitch|(`pitch`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setPitch|(`pitch`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {pitch} (`number`)
            Calculated with regard to 1 being the base pitch. Each reduction by
            50 percent equals a pitch shift of -12 semitones (one octave
            reduction). Each doubling equals a pitch shift of 12 semitones (one
            octave increase). Zero is not a legal value.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             *LOVE-Source:setPosition* *love-Source:setPosition*
|Source:setPosition|

Sets the position of the Source. Please note that this only works for mono (i.e.
non-stereo) sound files!

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setPosition|(`x`, `y`, `z`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setPosition|(`x`, `y`, `z`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The X position of the Source.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The Y position of the Source.
        • {z} (`number`)
            The Z position of the Source.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             *LOVE-Source:setRelative* *love-Source:setRelative*
|Source:setRelative|

Sets whether the Source's position, velocity, direction, and cone angles are
relative to the listener, or absolute.

By default, all sources are absolute and therefore relative to the origin of
love's coordinate system 0, 0. Only absolute sources are affected by the
position of the listener. Please note that positional audio only works for mono
(i.e. non-stereo) sources.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setRelative|(`enable`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setRelative|(`enable`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {enable} (`boolean`) (default: `false`)
            True to make the position, velocity, direction and cone angles
            relative to the listener, false to make them absolute.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                               *LOVE-Source:setRolloff* *love-Source:setRolloff*
|Source:setRolloff|

Sets the rolloff factor which affects the strength of the used distance attenuation.

Extended information and detailed formulas can be found in the chapter '3.4.
Attenuation By Distance' of OpenAL 1.1 specification.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setRolloff|(`rolloff`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setRolloff|(`rolloff`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {rolloff} (`number`)
            The new rolloff factor.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             *LOVE-Source:setVelocity* *love-Source:setVelocity*
|Source:setVelocity|

Sets the velocity of the Source.

This does '''not''' change the position of the Source, but lets the application
know how it has to calculate the doppler effect.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setVelocity|(`x`, `y`, `z`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setVelocity|(`x`, `y`, `z`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The X part of the velocity vector.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The Y part of the velocity vector.
        • {z} (`number`)
            The Z part of the velocity vector.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-Source:setVolume* *love-Source:setVolume*
|Source:setVolume|

Sets the current volume of the Source.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setVolume|(`volume`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setVolume|(`volume`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {volume} (`number`)
            The volume for a Source, where 1.0 is normal volume. Volume cannot
            be raised above 1.0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Source:setVolumeLimits* *love-Source:setVolumeLimits*
|Source:setVolumeLimits|

Sets the volume limits of the source. The limits have to be numbers from 0 to 1.

Synopses: ~
    |Source:setVolumeLimits|(`min`, `max`)

Variant: ~
    |Source:setVolumeLimits|(`min`, `max`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {min} (`number`)
            The minimum volume.
        • {max} (`number`)
            The maximum volume.

                                               *LOVE-love.data* *love-love.data*
|love.data|

Provides functionality for creating and transforming data.

    |callbacks|                                       |LOVE-love.data-callbacks|
    |enums|                                               |LOVE-love.data-enums|
    |functions|                                       |LOVE-love.data-functions|
    |types|                                               |LOVE-love.data-types|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-love.data-callbacks* *love-love.data-callbacks*
The callbacks of |love.data|: ~

    None

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.data-enums* *love-love.data-enums*
The enums of |love.data|: ~

    |CompressedDataFormat|                           |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
    |ContainerType|                                         |LOVE-ContainerType|
    |EncodeFormat|                                           |LOVE-EncodeFormat|
    |HashFunction|                                           |LOVE-HashFunction|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-CompressedDataFormat* *love-CompressedDataFormat*
|CompressedDataFormat|

Compressed data formats.

    Constants: ~

        • {"lz4"} (`string`)
            The LZ4 compression format. Compresses and decompresses very
            quickly, but the compression ratio is not the best. LZ4-HC is used
            when compression level 9 is specified. Some benchmarks are available
            here.

        • {"zlib"} (`string`)
            The zlib format is DEFLATE-compressed data with a small bit of
            header data. Compresses relatively slowly and decompresses
            moderately quickly, and has a decent compression ratio.

        • {"gzip"} (`string`)
            The gzip format is DEFLATE-compressed data with a slightly larger
            header than zlib. Since it uses DEFLATE it has the same compression
            characteristics as the zlib format.

        • {"deflate"} (`string`)
            Raw DEFLATE-compressed data (no header).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-ContainerType* *love-ContainerType*
|ContainerType|

Return type of various data-returning functions.

    Constants: ~

        • {"data"} (`string`)
            Return type is ByteData.

        • {"string"} (`string`)
            Return type is string.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                         *LOVE-EncodeFormat* *love-EncodeFormat*
|EncodeFormat|

Encoding format used to encode or decode data.

    Constants: ~

        • {"base64"} (`string`)
            Encode/decode data as base64 binary-to-text encoding.

        • {"hex"} (`string`)
            Encode/decode data as hexadecimal string.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                         *LOVE-HashFunction* *love-HashFunction*
|HashFunction|

Hash algorithm of love.data.hash.

    Constants: ~

        • {"md5"} (`string`)
            MD5 hash algorithm (16 bytes).

        • {"sha1"} (`string`)
            SHA1 hash algorithm (20 bytes).

        • {"sha224"} (`string`)
            SHA2 hash algorithm with message digest size of 224 bits (28 bytes).

        • {"sha256"} (`string`)
            SHA2 hash algorithm with message digest size of 256 bits (32 bytes).

        • {"sha384"} (`string`)
            SHA2 hash algorithm with message digest size of 384 bits (48 bytes).

        • {"sha512"} (`string`)
            SHA2 hash algorithm with message digest size of 512 bits (64 bytes).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             *LOVE-love.data.compress* *love-love.data.compress*
|love.data.compress|

Compresses a string or data using a specific compression algorithm.

Synopses: ~
    1. `compressedData` = |love.data.compress|(`container`, `format`,
    `rawstring`, `level`)
    2. `compressedData` = |love.data.compress|(`container`, `format`,
    `data`, `level`)

Variants: ~
    1. `compressedData` = |love.data.compress|(`container`, `format`,
    `rawstring`, `level`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the compressed data as.
        • {format} (`CompressedDataFormat`) |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
            The format to use when compressing the string.
        • {rawstring} (`string`)
            The raw (un-compressed) string to compress.
        • {level} (`number`) (default: `-1`)
            The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the
            default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the
            compression format being used.

    Return: ~
        (`CompressedDataorstring`) `compressedData`
            CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of
            rawstring.

    2. `compressedData` = |love.data.compress|(`container`, `format`,
    `data`, `level`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the compressed data as.
        • {format} (`CompressedDataFormat`) |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
            The format to use when compressing the data.
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            A Data object containing the raw (un-compressed) data to compress.
        • {level} (`number`) (default: `-1`)
            The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the
            default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the
            compression format being used.

    Return: ~
        (`CompressedDataorstring`) `compressedData`
            CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-love.data.decode* *love-love.data.decode*
|love.data.decode|

Decode Data or a string from any of the EncodeFormats to Data or string.

Synopses: ~
    1. `decoded` = |love.data.decode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceString`)
    2. `decoded` = |love.data.decode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceData`)

Variants: ~
    1. `decoded` = |love.data.decode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceString`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the decoded data as.
        • {format} (`EncodeFormat`) |LOVE-EncodeFormat|
            The format of the input data.
        • {sourceString} (`string`)
            The raw (encoded) data to decode.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteDataorstring`) `decoded`
            ByteData/string which contains the decoded version of source.

    2. `decoded` = |love.data.decode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceData`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the decoded data as.
        • {format} (`EncodeFormat`) |LOVE-EncodeFormat|
            The format of the input data.
        • {sourceData} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The raw (encoded) data to decode.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteDataorstring`) `decoded`
            ByteData/string which contains the decoded version of source.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-love.data.decompress* *love-love.data.decompress*
|love.data.decompress|

Decompresses a CompressedData or previously compressed string or Data object.

Synopses: ~
    1. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`,
    `compressedData`)
    2. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`, `format`,
    `compressedString`)
    3. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`, `format`,
    `data`)

Variants: ~
    1. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`,
    `compressedData`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the decompressed data as.
        • {compressedData} (`CompressedData`) |LOVE-CompressedData|
            The compressed data to decompress.

    Return: ~
        (`Dataorstring`) `decompressedData`
            Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

    2. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`, `format`,
    `compressedString`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the decompressed data as.
        • {format} (`CompressedDataFormat`) |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
            The format that was used to compress the given string.
        • {compressedString} (`string`)
            A string containing data previously compressed with love.data.compress.

    Return: ~
        (`Dataorstring`) `decompressedData`
            Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

    3. `decompressedData` = |love.data.decompress|(`container`, `format`,
    `data`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the decompressed data as.
        • {format} (`CompressedDataFormat`) |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
            The format that was used to compress the given data.
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            A Data object containing data previously compressed with
            love.data.compress.

    Return: ~
        (`Dataorstring`) `decompressedData`
            Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-love.data.encode* *love-love.data.encode*
|love.data.encode|

Encode Data or a string to a Data or string in one of the EncodeFormats.

Synopses: ~
    1. `encoded` = |love.data.encode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceString`,
    `linelength`)
    2. `encoded` = |love.data.encode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceData`,
    `linelength`)

Variants: ~
    1. `encoded` = |love.data.encode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceString`,
    `linelength`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the encoded data as.
        • {format} (`EncodeFormat`) |LOVE-EncodeFormat|
            The format of the output data.
        • {sourceString} (`string`)
            The raw data to encode.
        • {linelength} (`number`) (default: `0`)
            The maximum line length of the output. Only supported for base64,
            ignored if 0.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteDataorstring`) `encoded`
            ByteData/string which contains the encoded version of source.

    2. `encoded` = |love.data.encode|(`container`, `format`, `sourceData`,
    `linelength`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the encoded data as.
        • {format} (`EncodeFormat`) |LOVE-EncodeFormat|
            The format of the output data.
        • {sourceData} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The raw data to encode.
        • {linelength} (`number`) (default: `0`)
            The maximum line length of the output. Only supported for base64,
            ignored if 0.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteDataorstring`) `encoded`
            ByteData/string which contains the encoded version of source.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-love.data.getPackedSize* *love-love.data.getPackedSize*
|love.data.getPackedSize|

Gets the size in bytes that a given format used with love.data.pack will use.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.packsize.

Synopses: ~
    `size` = |love.data.getPackedSize|(`format`)

Variant: ~
    `size` = |love.data.getPackedSize|(`format`)

    The format string cannot have the variable-length options 's' or 'z'.

    Parameters: ~
        • {format} (`string`)
            A string determining how the values are packed. Follows the rules of
            Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `size`
            The size in bytes that the packed data will use.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-love.data.hash* *love-love.data.hash*
|love.data.hash|

Compute the message digest of a string using a specified hash algorithm.

Synopses: ~
    1. `rawdigest` = |love.data.hash|(`hashFunction`, `string`)
    2. `rawdigest` = |love.data.hash|(`hashFunction`, `data`)

Variants: ~
    1. `rawdigest` = |love.data.hash|(`hashFunction`, `string`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {hashFunction} (`HashFunction`) |LOVE-HashFunction|
            Hash algorithm to use.
        • {string} (`string`)
            String to hash.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `rawdigest`
            Raw message digest string.

    2. `rawdigest` = |love.data.hash|(`hashFunction`, `data`)

    To return the hex string representation of the hash, use love.data.encode

    hexDigestString = love.data.encode('string', 'hex', love.data.hash(algo, data))

    Parameters: ~
        • {hashFunction} (`HashFunction`) |LOVE-HashFunction|
            Hash algorithm to use.
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            Data to hash.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `rawdigest`
            Raw message digest string.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.data.newByteData* *love-love.data.newByteData*
|love.data.newByteData|

Creates a new Data object containing arbitrary bytes.

Data:getPointer along with LuaJIT's FFI can be used to manipulate the contents
of the ByteData object after it has been created.

Synopses: ~
    1. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`datastring`)
    2. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`Data`, `offset`, `size`)
    3. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`size`)

Variants: ~
    1. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`datastring`)

    Creates a new ByteData by copying the contents of the specified string.

    Parameters: ~
        • {datastring} (`string`)
            The byte string to copy.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteData`) `bytedata` |LOVE-ByteData|
            The new Data object.

    2. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`Data`, `offset`, `size`)

    Creates a new ByteData by copying from an existing Data object.

    Parameters: ~
        • {Data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The existing Data object to copy.
        • {offset} (`number`) (default: `0`)
            The offset of the subsection to copy, in bytes.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `data:getSize()`)
            The size in bytes of the new Data object.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteData`) `bytedata` |LOVE-ByteData|
            The new Data object.

    3. `bytedata` = |love.data.newByteData|(`size`)

    Creates a new empty ByteData with the specific size.

    Parameters: ~
        • {size} (`number`)
            The size in bytes of the new Data object.

    Return: ~
        (`ByteData`) `bytedata` |LOVE-ByteData|
            The new Data object.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.data.newDataView* *love-love.data.newDataView*
|love.data.newDataView|

Creates a new Data referencing a subsection of an existing Data object.

Synopses: ~
    `view` = |love.data.newDataView|(`data`, `offset`, `size`)

Variant: ~
    `view` = |love.data.newDataView|(`data`, `offset`, `size`)

    Data:getString and Data:getPointer will return the original Data object's
    contents, with the view's offset and size applied.

    Parameters: ~
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The Data object to reference.
        • {offset} (`number`)
            The offset of the subsection to reference, in bytes.
        • {size} (`number`)
            The size in bytes of the subsection to reference.

    Return: ~
        (`Data`) `view` |LOVE-Data|
            The new Data view.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-love.data.pack* *love-love.data.pack*
|love.data.pack|

Packs (serializes) simple Lua values.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.pack.

Synopses: ~
    `data` = |love.data.pack|(`container`, `format`, `v1`, `...`)

Variant: ~
    `data` = |love.data.pack|(`container`, `format`, `v1`, `...`)

    Packing integers with values greater than 2^52 is not supported, as Lua 5.1
    cannot represent those values in its number type.

    Parameters: ~
        • {container} (`ContainerType`) |LOVE-ContainerType|
            What type to return the encoded data as.
        • {format} (`string`)
            A string determining how the values are packed. Follows the rules of
            Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.
        • {v1} (`numberorbooleanorstring`)
            The first value (number, boolean, or string) to serialize.
        • {...} (`numberorbooleanorstring`)
            Additional values to serialize.

    Return: ~
        (`Dataorstring`) `data`
            Data/string which contains the serialized data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-love.data.unpack* *love-love.data.unpack*
|love.data.unpack|

Unpacks (deserializes) a byte-string or Data into simple Lua values.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.unpack.

Synopses: ~
    1. `v1`, `...`, `index` = |love.data.unpack|(`format`, `datastring`, `pos`)
    2. `v1`, `...`, `index` = |love.data.unpack|(`format`, `data`, `pos`)

Variants: ~
    1. `v1`, `...`, `index` = |love.data.unpack|(`format`, `datastring`, `pos`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {format} (`string`)
            A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules
            of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.
        • {datastring} (`string`)
            A string containing the packed (serialized) data.
        • {pos} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            Where to start reading in the string. Negative values can be used to
            read relative from the end of the string.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`numberorbooleanorstring`) `v1`
            The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.

        2. (`numberorbooleanorstring`) `...`
            Additional unpacked values.

        3. (`number`) `index`
            The index of the first unread byte in the data string.

    2. `v1`, `...`, `index` = |love.data.unpack|(`format`, `data`, `pos`)

    Unpacking integers with values greater than 2^52 is not supported, as Lua
    5.1 cannot represent those values in its number type.

    Parameters: ~
        • {format} (`string`)
            A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules
            of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            A Data object containing the packed (serialized) data.
        • {pos} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            1-based index indicating where to start reading in the Data.
            Negative values can be used to read relative from the end of the
            Data object.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`numberorbooleanorstring`) `v1`
            The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.

        2. (`numberorbooleanorstring`) `...`
            Additional unpacked values.

        3. (`number`) `index`
            The 1-based index of the first unread byte in the Data.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.data-types* *love-love.data-types*
The types of |love.data|: ~

    |ByteData|                                                   |LOVE-ByteData|
    |CompressedData|                                       |LOVE-CompressedData|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                 *LOVE-ByteData* *love-ByteData*
|ByteData|

Data object containing arbitrary bytes in an contiguous memory.

There are currently no LÖVE functions provided for manipulating the contents of
a ByteData, but Data:getPointer can be used with LuaJIT's FFI to access and
write to the contents directly.

                       *LOVE-ByteData-constructors* *love-ByteData-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        |newByteData|                                         |LOVE-newByteData|

                           *LOVE-ByteData-supertypes* *love-ByteData-supertypes*
    Supertypes: ~
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|
        |Data|                                                       |LOVE-Data|

                               *LOVE-ByteData-subtypes* *love-ByteData-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                             *LOVE-ByteData-functions* *love-ByteData-functions*
    Functions: ~
        None

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-CompressedData* *love-CompressedData*
|CompressedData|

Represents byte data compressed using a specific algorithm.

love.data.decompress can be used to de-compress the data (or
love.math.decompress in 0.10.2 or earlier).

           *LOVE-CompressedData-constructors* *love-CompressedData-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        |compress|                                               |LOVE-compress|

               *LOVE-CompressedData-supertypes* *love-CompressedData-supertypes*
    Supertypes: ~
        |Data|                                                       |LOVE-Data|
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|

                   *LOVE-CompressedData-subtypes* *love-CompressedData-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                 *LOVE-CompressedData-functions* *love-CompressedData-functions*
    Functions: ~
        |getFormat|                              |LOVE-CompressedData:getFormat|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-CompressedData:getFormat* *love-CompressedData:getFormat*
|CompressedData:getFormat|

Gets the compression format of the CompressedData.

Synopses: ~
    `format` = |CompressedData:getFormat|()

Variant: ~
    `format` = |CompressedData:getFormat|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`CompressedDataFormat`) `format` |LOVE-CompressedDataFormat|
            The format of the CompressedData.

|love.event|

Manages events, like keypresses.

    |callbacks|                                      |LOVE-love.event-callbacks|
    |enums|                                              |LOVE-love.event-enums|
    |functions|                                      |LOVE-love.event-functions|
    |types|                                              |LOVE-love.event-types|

                         *LOVE-love.event-callbacks* *love-love.event-callbacks*

    None

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-love.event-enums* *love-love.event-enums*
The enums of |love.event|: ~

    |Event|                                                         |LOVE-Event|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                       *LOVE-Event* *love-Event*
|Event|

Arguments to love.event.push() and the like.

Since 0.8.0, event names are no longer abbreviated.

    Constants: ~

        • {"focus"} (`string`)
            Window focus gained or lost

        • {"joystickpressed"} (`string`)
            Joystick pressed

        • {"joystickreleased"} (`string`)
            Joystick released

        • {"keypressed"} (`string`)
            Key pressed

        • {"keyreleased"} (`string`)
            Key released

        • {"mousepressed"} (`string`)
            Mouse pressed

        • {"mousereleased"} (`string`)
            Mouse released

        • {"quit"} (`string`)
            Quit

        • {"resize"} (`string`)
            Window size changed by the user

        • {"visible"} (`string`)
            Window is minimized or un-minimized by the user

        • {"mousefocus"} (`string`)
            Window mouse focus gained or lost

        • {"threaderror"} (`string`)
            A Lua error has occurred in a thread

        • {"joystickadded"} (`string`)
            Joystick connected

        • {"joystickremoved"} (`string`)
            Joystick disconnected

        • {"joystickaxis"} (`string`)
            Joystick axis motion

        • {"joystickhat"} (`string`)
            Joystick hat pressed

        • {"gamepadpressed"} (`string`)
            Joystick's virtual gamepad button pressed

        • {"gamepadreleased"} (`string`)
            Joystick's virtual gamepad button released

        • {"gamepadaxis"} (`string`)
            Joystick's virtual gamepad axis moved

        • {"textinput"} (`string`)
            User entered text

        • {"mousemoved"} (`string`)
            Mouse position changed

        • {"lowmemory"} (`string`)
            Running out of memory on mobile devices system

        • {"textedited"} (`string`)
            Candidate text for an IME changed

        • {"wheelmoved"} (`string`)
            Mouse wheel moved

        • {"touchpressed"} (`string`)
            Touch screen touched

        • {"touchreleased"} (`string`)
            Touch screen stop touching

        • {"touchmoved"} (`string`)
            Touch press moved inside touch screen

        • {"directorydropped"} (`string`)
            Directory is dragged and dropped onto the window

        • {"filedropped"} (`string`)
            File is dragged and dropped onto the window.

        • {"jp"} (`string`)
            Joystick pressed

        • {"jr"} (`string`)
            Joystick released

        • {"kp"} (`string`)
            Key pressed

        • {"kr"} (`string`)
            Key released

        • {"mp"} (`string`)
            Mouse pressed

        • {"mr"} (`string`)
            Mouse released

        • {"q"} (`string`)
            Quit

        • {"f"} (`string`)
            Window focus gained or lost

                                   *LOVE-love.event.poll* *love-love.event.poll*

    See function description

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.event.pump* *love-love.event.pump*
|love.event.pump|

Pump events into the event queue.

This is a low-level function, and is usually not called by the user, but by
love.run.

Note that this does need to be called for any OS to think you're still running,

and if you want to handle OS-generated events at all (think callbacks).

Synopses: ~
    |love.event.pump|()

Variant: ~
    |love.event.pump|()

    See function description

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.event.push* *love-love.event.push*
|love.event.push|

Adds an event to the event queue.

From 0.10.0 onwards, you may pass an arbitrary amount of arguments with this
function, though the default callbacks don't ever use more than six.

Synopses: ~
    |love.event.push|(`n`, `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`, `f`, `...`)

Variant: ~
    |love.event.push|(`n`, `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`, `f`, `...`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {n} (`Event`) |LOVE-Event|
            The name of the event.
        • {a} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            First event argument.
        • {b} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Second event argument.
        • {c} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Third event argument.
        • {d} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Fourth event argument.
        • {e} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Fifth event argument.
        • {f} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Sixth event argument.
        • {...} (`Variant`) (default: `nil`)
            Further event arguments may follow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.event.quit* *love-love.event.quit*
|love.event.quit|

Adds the quit event to the queue.

The quit event is a signal for the event handler to close LÖVE. It's possible
to abort the exit process with the love.quit callback.

Synopses: ~
    1. |love.event.quit|(`exitstatus`)
    2. |love.event.quit|(`'restart'`)

Variants: ~
    1. |love.event.quit|(`exitstatus`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {exitstatus} (`number`) (default: `0`)
            The program exit status to use when closing the application.

    2. |love.event.quit|(`'restart'`)

    Restarts the game without relaunching the executable. This cleanly shuts
    down the main Lua state instance and creates a brand new one.

    Parameters: ~
        • {'restart'} (`string`)
            Tells the default love.run to exit and restart the game without
            relaunching the executable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.event.wait* *love-love.event.wait*
|love.event.wait|

Like love.event.poll(), but blocks until there is an event in the queue.

Synopses: ~
    `n`, `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`, `f`, `...` = |love.event.wait|()

Variant: ~
    `n`, `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`, `f`, `...` = |love.event.wait|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`Event`) `n` |LOVE-Event|
            The name of event.

        2. (`Variant`) `a`
            First event argument.

        3. (`Variant`) `b`
            Second event argument.

        4. (`Variant`) `c`
            Third event argument.

        5. (`Variant`) `d`
            Fourth event argument.

        6. (`Variant`) `e`
            Fifth event argument.

        7. (`Variant`) `f`
            Sixth event argument.

        8. (`Variant`) `...`
            Further event arguments may follow.

    |callbacks|                                 |LOVE-love.filesystem-callbacks|

    |functions|                                 |LOVE-love.filesystem-functions|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.filesystem-enums* *love-love.filesystem-enums*
The enums of |love.filesystem|: ~

    |BufferMode|                                               |LOVE-BufferMode|
    |FileDecoder|                                             |LOVE-FileDecoder|
    |FileMode|                                                   |LOVE-FileMode|
    |FileType|                                                   |LOVE-FileType|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                             *LOVE-BufferMode* *love-BufferMode*
|BufferMode|

Buffer modes for File objects.

    Constants: ~

        • {"none"} (`string`)
            No buffering. The result of write and append operations appears
            immediately.

        • {"line"} (`string`)
            Line buffering. Write and append operations are buffered until a
            newline is output or the buffer size limit is reached.

        • {"full"} (`string`)
            Full buffering. Write and append operations are always buffered
            until the buffer size limit is reached.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                           *LOVE-FileDecoder* *love-FileDecoder*
|FileDecoder|

How to decode a given FileData.

    Constants: ~

        • {"file"} (`string`)
            The data is unencoded.

        • {"base64"} (`string`)
            The data is base64-encoded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                 *LOVE-FileMode* *love-FileMode*
|FileMode|

The different modes you can open a File in.

    Constants: ~

        • {"r"} (`string`)
            Open a file for read.

        • {"w"} (`string`)
            Open a file for write.

        • {"a"} (`string`)
            Open a file for append.

        • {"c"} (`string`)
            Do not open a file (represents a closed file.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                 *LOVE-FileType* *love-FileType*
|FileType|

The type of a file.

    Constants: ~

        • {"file"} (`string`)
            Regular file.

        • {"directory"} (`string`)
            Directory.

        • {"symlink"} (`string`)
            Symbolic link.

        • {"other"} (`string`)
            Something completely different like a device.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   *LOVE-love.filesystem.getCRequirePath* *love-love.filesystem.getCRequirePath*
|love.filesystem.getCRequirePath|

Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched for c libraries when require is
called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates
separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in
place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot
characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory
separators.) Additionally, any occurrence of a double question mark ('??') will
be replaced by the name passed to require and the default library extension for
the platform.

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any
paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

Synopses: ~
    `paths` = |love.filesystem.getCRequirePath|()

Variant: ~
    `paths` = |love.filesystem.getCRequirePath|()

    The default paths string is '??', which makes require('cool') try to load
    cool.dll, or cool.so depending on the platform.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `paths`
            The paths that the ''require'' function will check for c libraries
            in love's filesystem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                        *LOVE-love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems*
                                        *love-love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems*
|love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems|

Returns a table with the names of files and subdirectories in the specified
path. The table is not sorted in any way; the order is undefined.

If the path passed to the function exists in the game and the save directory, it
will list the files and directories from both places.

Synopses: ~
    1. `files` = |love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems|(`dir`)
    2. `files` = |love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems|(`dir`, `callback`)

Variants: ~
    1. `files` = |love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems|(`dir`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {dir} (`string`)
            The directory.

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `files`
            A sequence with the names of all files and subdirectories as strings.

    2. `files` = |love.filesystem.getDirectoryItems|(`dir`, `callback`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {dir} (`string`)
            The directory.
        • {callback} (`function`)
            A function which is called for each file and folder in the
            directory. The filename is passed to the function as an argument.

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `files`
            A sequence with the names of all files and subdirectories as strings.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

                   *LOVE-love.filesystem.getInfo* *love-love.filesystem.getInfo*

    See function description

            The file or directory path to check.

            If supplied, this parameter causes getInfo to only return the info
            table if the item at the given path matches the specified file type.

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `info`
            A table containing information about the specified path, or nil if
            nothing exists at the path. The table contains the following fields:

            The file or directory path to check.
        • {info} (`table`)
            A table which will be filled in with info about the specified path.

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `info`
            The table given as an argument, or nil if nothing exists at the
            path. The table will be filled in with the following fields:

    3. `info` = |love.filesystem.getInfo|(`path`, `filtertype`, `info`)

    This variant only returns info if the item at the given path is the same
    file type as specified in the filtertype argument, and accepts an existing
    table to fill in, instead of creating a new one.

    Parameters: ~
        • {path} (`string`)
            The file or directory path to check.
        • {filtertype} (`FileType`) |LOVE-FileType|
            Causes getInfo to only return the info table if the item at the
            given path matches the specified file type.
        • {info} (`table`)
            A table which will be filled in with info about the specified path.

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `info`
            The table given as an argument, or nil if nothing exists at the
            path. The table will be filled in with the following fields:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *LOVE-love.filesystem.getRealDirectory* *love-love.filesystem.getRealDirectory*
|love.filesystem.getRealDirectory|

Gets the platform-specific absolute path of the directory containing a filepath.

This can be used to determine whether a file is inside the save directory or the
game's source .love.

Synopses: ~
    `realdir` = |love.filesystem.getRealDirectory|(`filepath`)

Variant: ~
    `realdir` = |love.filesystem.getRealDirectory|(`filepath`)

    This function returns the directory containing the given ''file path'',
    rather than file. For example, if the file screenshot1.png exists in a
    directory called screenshots in the game's save directory,
    love.filesystem.getRealDirectory('screenshots/screenshot1.png') will return
    the same value as love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory.

    Parameters: ~
        • {filepath} (`string`)
            The filepath to get the directory of.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `realdir`
            The platform-specific full path of the directory containing the
            filepath.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     *LOVE-love.filesystem.getRequirePath* *love-love.filesystem.getRequirePath*
|love.filesystem.getRequirePath|

Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates
separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in
place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot
characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory
separators.)

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any
paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

Synopses: ~
    `paths` = |love.filesystem.getRequirePath|()

Variant: ~
    `paths` = |love.filesystem.getRequirePath|()

    The default paths string is '?.lua;?/init.lua', which makes require('cool')
    try to load cool.lua and then try cool/init.lua if cool.lua doesn't exist.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `paths`
            The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *LOVE-love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory* *love-love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory*
|love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory|

Gets the full path to the designated save directory.

This can be useful if you want to use the standard io library (or something
else) to

read or write in the save directory.

Synopses: ~
    `dir` = |love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory|()

    `dir` = |love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `dir`
            The absolute path to the save directory.

               *LOVE-love.filesystem.getSource* *love-love.filesystem.getSource*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory*
                                   *love-love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory*
|love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory|

Returns the full path to the directory containing the .love file. If the game is
fused to the LÖVE executable, then the directory containing the executable is
returned.

If love.filesystem.isFused is true, the path returned by this function can be
passed to love.filesystem.mount, which will make the directory containing the
main game (e.g. C:\Program Files\coolgame\) readable by love.filesystem.

Synopses: ~
    `path` = |love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory|()

Variant: ~
    `path` = |love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `path`
            The full platform-dependent path of the directory containing the
            .love file.

                         *LOVE-love.filesystem.init* *love-love.filesystem.init*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-love.filesystem.isFused* *love-love.filesystem.isFused*
|love.filesystem.isFused|

Gets whether the game is in fused mode or not.

If a game is in fused mode, its save directory will be directly in the Appdata
directory instead of Appdata/LOVE/. The game will also be able to load C Lua
dynamic libraries which are located in the save directory.

A game is in fused mode if the source .love has been fused to the executable
(see Game Distribution), or if '--fused' has been given as a command-line
argument when starting the game.

Synopses: ~
    `fused` = |love.filesystem.isFused|()

Variant: ~
    `fused` = |love.filesystem.isFused|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `fused`
            True if the game is in fused mode, false otherwise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.filesystem.lines* *love-love.filesystem.lines*
|love.filesystem.lines|

    `iterator` = |love.filesystem.lines|(`name`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~

            The name (and path) of the file

    Return: ~

            A function that iterates over all the lines in the file

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.filesystem.mount* *love-love.filesystem.mount*
|love.filesystem.mount|

Mounts a zip file or folder in the game's save directory for reading.

It is also possible to mount love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory if the game
is in fused mode.

Synopses: ~
    1. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`archive`, `mountpoint`,
    `appendToPath`)
    2. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`filedata`, `mountpoint`,
    `appendToPath`)
    3. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`data`, `archivename`,
    `mountpoint`, `appendToPath`)

Variants: ~
    1. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`archive`, `mountpoint`,
    `appendToPath`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {archive} (`string`)
            The folder or zip file in the game's save directory to mount.
        • {mountpoint} (`string`)
            The new path the archive will be mounted to.
        • {appendToPath} (`boolean`) (default: `false`)
            Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before
            or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source
            and save directories.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `success`
            True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

    2. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`filedata`, `mountpoint`,
    `appendToPath`)

    Mounts the contents of the given FileData in memory. The FileData's data
    must contain a zipped directory structure.

    Parameters: ~
        • {filedata} (`FileData`) |LOVE-FileData|
            The FileData object in memory to mount.
        • {mountpoint} (`string`)
            The new path the archive will be mounted to.
        • {appendToPath} (`boolean`) (default: `false`)
            Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before
            or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source
            and save directories.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `success`
            True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

    3. `success` = |love.filesystem.mount|(`data`, `archivename`,
    `mountpoint`, `appendToPath`)

    Mounts the contents of the given Data object in memory. The data must
    contain a zipped directory structure.

    Parameters: ~
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The Data object in memory to mount.
        • {archivename} (`string`)
            The name to associate the mounted data with, for use with
            love.filesystem.unmount. Must be unique compared to other mounted data.
        • {mountpoint} (`string`)
            The new path the archive will be mounted to.
        • {appendToPath} (`boolean`) (default: `false`)
            Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before
            or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source
            and save directories.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `success`
            True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-love.filesystem.newFile* *love-love.filesystem.newFile*
|love.filesystem.newFile|

Creates a new File object.

It needs to be opened before it can be accessed.

Synopses: ~
    1. `file` = |love.filesystem.newFile|(`filename`)
    2. `file`, `errorstr` = |love.filesystem.newFile|(`filename`, `mode`)

Variants: ~
    1. `file` = |love.filesystem.newFile|(`filename`)

    Please note that this function will not return any error message (e.g. if
    you use an invalid filename) because it just creates the File Object. You
    can still check if the file is valid by using File:open which returns a
    boolean and an error message if something goes wrong while opening the file.

    Parameters: ~
        • {filename} (`string`)
            The filename of the file.

    Return: ~
        (`File`) `file` |LOVE-File|
            The new File object.

    2. `file`, `errorstr` = |love.filesystem.newFile|(`filename`, `mode`)

    Creates a File object and opens it for reading, writing, or appending.

    Parameters: ~
        • {filename} (`string`)
            The filename of the file.
        • {mode} (`FileMode`) |LOVE-FileMode|
            The mode to open the file in.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`File`) `file` |LOVE-File|
            The new File object, or nil if an error occurred.

        2. (`string`) `errorstr`
            The error string if an error occurred.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
           *LOVE-love.filesystem.newFileData* *love-love.filesystem.newFileData*
|love.filesystem.newFileData|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-love.filesystem.remove* *love-love.filesystem.remove*
|love.filesystem.remove|

Removes a file or empty directory.

Synopses: ~
    `success` = |love.filesystem.remove|(`name`)

Variant: ~
    `success` = |love.filesystem.remove|(`name`)

    The directory must be empty before removal or else it will fail. Simply
    remove all files and folders in the directory beforehand.

    If the file exists in the .love but not in the save directory, it returns
    false as well.

    An opened File prevents removal of the underlying file. Simply close the
    File to remove it.

    Parameters: ~
        • {name} (`string`)
            The file or directory to remove.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `success`
            True if the file/directory was removed, false otherwise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   *LOVE-love.filesystem.setCRequirePath* *love-love.filesystem.setCRequirePath*
|love.filesystem.setCRequirePath|

Sets the filesystem paths that will be searched for c libraries when require is
called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates
separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in
place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot
characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory
separators.) Additionally, any occurrence of a double question mark ('??') will
be replaced by the name passed to require and the default library extension for
the platform.

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any
paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

Synopses: ~
    |love.filesystem.setCRequirePath|(`paths`)

Variant: ~
    |love.filesystem.setCRequirePath|(`paths`)

    The default paths string is '??', which makes require('cool') try to load
    cool.dll, or cool.so depending on the platform.

    Parameters: ~
        • {paths} (`string`)
            The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
           *LOVE-love.filesystem.setIdentity* *love-love.filesystem.setIdentity*
|love.filesystem.setIdentity|

Sets the write directory for your game.

Note that you can only set the name of the folder to store your files in, not
the location.

Synopses: ~
    1. |love.filesystem.setIdentity|(`name`)
    2. |love.filesystem.setIdentity|(`name`)

Variants: ~
    1. |love.filesystem.setIdentity|(`name`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {name} (`string`)
            The new identity that will be used as write directory.

    2. |love.filesystem.setIdentity|(`name`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {name} (`string`)
            The new identity that will be used as write directory.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     *LOVE-love.filesystem.setRequirePath* *love-love.filesystem.setRequirePath*
|love.filesystem.setRequirePath|

Sets the filesystem paths that will be searched when require is called.

The paths string given to this function is a sequence of path templates
separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in
place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot
characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory
separators.)

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any
paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

Synopses: ~
    |love.filesystem.setRequirePath|(`paths`)

Variant: ~
    |love.filesystem.setRequirePath|(`paths`)

    The default paths string is '?.lua;?/init.lua', which makes require('cool')
    try to load cool.lua and then try cool/init.lua if cool.lua doesn't exist.

    Parameters: ~
        • {paths} (`string`)
            The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
               *LOVE-love.filesystem.setSource* *love-love.filesystem.setSource*
|love.filesystem.setSource|

Sets the source of the game, where the code is present. This function can only
be called once, and is normally automatically done by LÖVE.

Synopses: ~
    |love.filesystem.setSource|(`path`)

Variant: ~
    |love.filesystem.setSource|(`path`)

    See function description

            Absolute path to the game's source folder.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.filesystem.write* *love-love.filesystem.write*
|love.filesystem.write|

Write data to a file in the save directory. If the file existed already, it will
be completely replaced by the new contents.

Synopses: ~
    1. `success`, `message` = |love.filesystem.write|(`name`, `data`, `size`)
    2. `success`, `message` = |love.filesystem.write|(`name`, `data`, `size`)

Variants: ~
    1. `success`, `message` = |love.filesystem.write|(`name`, `data`, `size`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {name} (`string`)
            The name (and path) of the file.
        • {data} (`string`)
            The string data to write to the file.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `all`)
            How many bytes to write.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`boolean`) `success`
            If the operation was successful.

        2. (`string`) `message`
            Error message if operation was unsuccessful.

    2. `success`, `message` = |love.filesystem.write|(`name`, `data`, `size`)

    If you are getting the error message 'Could not set write directory', try
    setting the save directory. This is done either with
    love.filesystem.setIdentity or by setting the identity field in love.conf.

    '''Writing to multiple lines''': In Windows, some text editors (e.g.
    Notepad) only treat CRLF ('\r\n') as a new line.

    Parameters: ~
        • {name} (`string`)
            The name (and path) of the file.
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The Data object to write to the file.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `all`)
            How many bytes to write.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`boolean`) `success`
            If the operation was successful.

        2. (`string`) `message`
            Error message if operation was unsuccessful.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-File:setBuffer* *love-File:setBuffer*
|File:setBuffer|

Sets the buffer mode for a file opened for writing or appending. Files with
buffering enabled will not write data to the disk until the buffer size limit is
reached, depending on the buffer mode.

File:flush will force any buffered data to be written to the disk.

Synopses: ~
    `success`, `errorstr` = |File:setBuffer|(`mode`, `size`)

Variant: ~
    `success`, `errorstr` = |File:setBuffer|(`mode`, `size`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {mode} (`BufferMode`) |LOVE-BufferMode|
            The buffer mode to use.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `0`)
            The maximum size in bytes of the file's buffer.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`boolean`) `success`
            Whether the buffer mode was successfully set.

        2. (`string`) `errorstr`
            The error string, if the buffer mode could not be set and an error
            occurred.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                               *LOVE-File:tell* *love-File:tell*
|File:tell|

Returns the position in the file.

Synopses: ~
    `pos` = |File:tell|()

Variant: ~
    `pos` = |File:tell|()

    See function description

    Return: ~

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                             *LOVE-File:write* *love-File:write*
|File:write|

Write data to a file.

Synopses: ~
    1. `success`, `err` = |File:write|(`data`, `size`)
    2. `success`, `errorstr` = |File:write|(`data`, `size`)

Variants: ~
    1. `success`, `err` = |File:write|(`data`, `size`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {data} (`string`)
            The string data to write.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `all`)
            How many bytes to write.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`boolean`) `success`
            Whether the operation was successful.

        2. (`string`) `err`
            The error string if an error occurred.

    2. `success`, `errorstr` = |File:write|(`data`, `size`)

    '''Writing to multiple lines''': In Windows, some text editors (e.g. Notepad
    before Windows 10 1809) only treat CRLF ('\r\n') as a new line.

    --example

    f = love.filesystem.newFile('note.txt')

    f:open('w')

    for i = 1, 10 do

        f:write('This is line '..i..'!\r\n')

    end

    f:close()

    Parameters: ~
        • {data} (`Data`) |LOVE-Data|
            The Data object to write.
        • {size} (`number`) (default: `all`)
            How many bytes to write.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`boolean`) `success`
            Whether the operation was successful.

        2. (`string`) `errorstr`
            The error string if an error occurred.

                                               *LOVE-love.font* *love-love.font*
|love.font|

Allows you to work with fonts.

    |callbacks|                                       |LOVE-love.font-callbacks|
    |enums|                                               |LOVE-love.font-enums|
    |functions|                                       |LOVE-love.font-functions|
    |types|                                               |LOVE-love.font-types|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-love.font-callbacks* *love-love.font-callbacks*
The callbacks of |love.font|: ~

    None

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-love.font-enums* *love-love.font-enums*
The enums of |love.font|: ~

    |HintingMode|                                             |LOVE-HintingMode|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                           *LOVE-HintingMode* *love-HintingMode*
|HintingMode|

True Type hinting mode.

    Constants: ~

        • {"normal"} (`string`)
            Default hinting. Should be preferred for typical antialiased fonts.

        • {"light"} (`string`)
            Results in fuzzier text but can sometimes preserve the original
            glyph shapes of the text better than normal hinting.

        • {"mono"} (`string`)
            Results in aliased / unsmoothed text with either full opacity or
            completely transparent pixels. Should be used when antialiasing is
            not desired for the font.

        • {"none"} (`string`)
            Disables hinting for the font. Results in fuzzier text.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-love.font-functions* *love-love.font-functions*
The functions of |love.font|: ~

    |newBMFontRasterizer|                   |LOVE-love.font.newBMFontRasterizer|

    |newImageRasterizer|                     |LOVE-love.font.newImageRasterizer|
    |newRasterizer|                               |LOVE-love.font.newRasterizer|
    |newTrueTypeRasterizer|               |LOVE-love.font.newTrueTypeRasterizer|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       *LOVE-love.font.newBMFontRasterizer* *love-love.font.newBMFontRasterizer*
|love.font.newBMFontRasterizer|

Creates a new BMFont Rasterizer.

Synopses: ~
    1. `rasterizer` = |love.font.newBMFontRasterizer|(`imageData`, `glyphs`,
    `dpiscale`)
    2. `rasterizer` = |love.font.newBMFontRasterizer|(`fileName`, `glyphs`,
    `dpiscale`)

Variants: ~
    1. `rasterizer` = |love.font.newBMFontRasterizer|(`imageData`, `glyphs`,
    `dpiscale`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~

            The image data containing the drawable pictures of font glyphs.

        • {dpiscale} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            DPI scale.

    Return: ~
        (`Rasterizer`) `rasterizer` |LOVE-Rasterizer|
            The rasterizer.

================================================================================
                                       *LOVE-love.graphics* *love-love.graphics*
|love.graphics|

The primary responsibility for the love.graphics module is the drawing of lines,
shapes, text, Images and other Drawable objects onto the screen. Its secondary
responsibilities include loading external files (including Images and Fonts)
into memory, creating specialized objects (such as ParticleSystems or Canvases)
and managing screen geometry.

LÖVE's coordinate system is rooted in the upper-left corner of the screen,
which is at location (0, 0). The x axis is horizontal: larger values are further
to the right. The y axis is vertical: larger values are further towards the bottom.

In many cases, you draw images or shapes in terms of their upper-left corner.

Many of the functions are used to manipulate the graphics coordinate system,
which is essentially the way coordinates are mapped to the display. You can
change the position, scale, and even rotation in this way.

    |callbacks|                                   |LOVE-love.graphics-callbacks|
    |enums|                                           |LOVE-love.graphics-enums|
    |functions|                                   |LOVE-love.graphics-functions|
    |types|                                           |LOVE-love.graphics-types|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-love.graphics-callbacks* *love-love.graphics-callbacks*
The callbacks of |love.graphics|: ~

    None
