--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.physics.setMeter* *love-love.physics.setMeter*
|love.physics.setMeter|

Sets the pixels to meter scale factor.

All coordinates in the physics module are divided by this number and converted
to meters, and it creates a convenient way to draw the objects directly to the
screen without the need for graphics transformations.

It is recommended to create shapes no larger than 10 times the scale. This is
important because Box2D is tuned to work well with shape sizes from 0.1 to 10
meters. The default meter scale is 30.

Synopses: ~
    |love.physics.setMeter|(`scale`)

Variant: ~
    |love.physics.setMeter|(`scale`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {scale} (`number`)
            The scale factor as an integer.

        |getY|                                                  |LOVE-Body:getY|
        |isActive|                                          |LOVE-Body:isActive|
        |isAwake|                                            |LOVE-Body:isAwake|
        |isBullet|                                          |LOVE-Body:isBullet|
        |isDestroyed|                                    |LOVE-Body:isDestroyed|
        |isFixedRotation|                            |LOVE-Body:isFixedRotation|
        |isSleepingAllowed|                        |LOVE-Body:isSleepingAllowed|
        |isTouching|                                      |LOVE-Body:isTouching|
        |resetMassData|                                |LOVE-Body:resetMassData|
        |setActive|                                        |LOVE-Body:setActive|
        |setAngle|                                          |LOVE-Body:setAngle|
        |setAngularDamping|                        |LOVE-Body:setAngularDamping|
        |setAngularVelocity|                      |LOVE-Body:setAngularVelocity|
        |setAwake|                                          |LOVE-Body:setAwake|
        |setBullet|                                        |LOVE-Body:setBullet|
        |setFixedRotation|                          |LOVE-Body:setFixedRotation|
        |setGravityScale|                            |LOVE-Body:setGravityScale|
        |setInertia|                                      |LOVE-Body:setInertia|
        |setLinearDamping|                          |LOVE-Body:setLinearDamping|
        |setLinearVelocity|                        |LOVE-Body:setLinearVelocity|
        |setMass|                                            |LOVE-Body:setMass|
        |setMassData|                                    |LOVE-Body:setMassData|
        |setPosition|                                    |LOVE-Body:setPosition|
        |setSleepingAllowed|                      |LOVE-Body:setSleepingAllowed|
        |setTransform|                                  |LOVE-Body:setTransform|
        |setType|                                            |LOVE-Body:setType|
        |setUserData|                                    |LOVE-Body:setUserData|
        |setX|                                                  |LOVE-Body:setX|
        |setY|                                                  |LOVE-Body:setY|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-Body:applyAngularImpulse* *love-Body:applyAngularImpulse*
|Body:applyAngularImpulse|

Applies an angular impulse to a body. This makes a single, instantaneous
addition to the body momentum.

A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction does '''not'''
depend on the timestep, and is equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1
second. Impulses are best used to give a single push to a body. For a continuous
push to a body it is better to use Body:applyForce.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:applyAngularImpulse|(`impulse`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:applyAngularImpulse|(`impulse`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {impulse} (`number`)
            The impulse in kilogram-square meter per second.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-Body:applyForce* *love-Body:applyForce*
|Body:applyForce|

Apply force to a Body.

A force pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will react
less. The reaction also depends on how long a force is applied: since the force
acts continuously over the entire timestep, a short timestep will only push the
body for a short time. Thus forces are best used for many timesteps to give a
continuous push to a body (like gravity). For a single push that is independent
of timestep, it is better to use Body:applyLinearImpulse.

If the position to apply the force is not given, it will act on the center of
mass of the body. The part of the force not directed towards the center of mass
will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia).

Note that the force components and position must be given in world coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    1. |Body:applyForce|(`fx`, `fy`)
    2. |Body:applyForce|(`fx`, `fy`, `x`, `y`)

Variants: ~
    1. |Body:applyForce|(`fx`, `fy`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {fx} (`number`)
            The x component of force to apply to the center of mass.
        • {fy} (`number`)
            The y component of force to apply to the center of mass.

    2. |Body:applyForce|(`fx`, `fy`, `x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {fx} (`number`)
            The x component of force to apply.
        • {fy} (`number`)
            The y component of force to apply.
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position to apply the force.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position to apply the force.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-Body:applyLinearImpulse* *love-Body:applyLinearImpulse*
|Body:applyLinearImpulse|

Applies an impulse to a body.

This makes a single, instantaneous addition to the body momentum.

An impulse pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will
react less. The reaction does '''not''' depend on the timestep, and is
equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1 second. Impulses are best used
to give a single push to a body. For a continuous push to a body it is better to
use Body:applyForce.

If the position to apply the impulse is not given, it will act on the center of
mass of the body. The part of the impulse not directed towards the center of
mass will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia).

Note that the impulse components and position must be given in world coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    1. |Body:applyLinearImpulse|(`ix`, `iy`)
    2. |Body:applyLinearImpulse|(`ix`, `iy`, `x`, `y`)

Variants: ~
    1. |Body:applyLinearImpulse|(`ix`, `iy`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {ix} (`number`)
            The x component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.
        • {iy} (`number`)
            The y component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.

    2. |Body:applyLinearImpulse|(`ix`, `iy`, `x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {ix} (`number`)
            The x component of the impulse.
        • {iy} (`number`)
            The y component of the impulse.
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position to apply the impulse.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position to apply the impulse.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-Body:applyTorque* *love-Body:applyTorque*
|Body:applyTorque|

Apply torque to a body.

Torque is like a force that will change the angular velocity (spin) of a body.
The effect will depend on the rotational inertia a body has.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:applyTorque|(`torque`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:applyTorque|(`torque`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {torque} (`number`)
            The torque to apply.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                         *LOVE-Body:destroy* *love-Body:destroy*
|Body:destroy|

Explicitly destroys the Body and all fixtures and joints attached to it.

An error will occur if you attempt to use the object after calling this
function. In 0.7.2, when you don't have time to wait for garbage collection,
this function may be used to free the object immediately.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:destroy|()

Variant: ~
    |Body:destroy|()

    See function description

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Body:getAngle* *love-Body:getAngle*
|Body:getAngle|

Get the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it to degrees, use
math.deg.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase
counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our
point of view.

Synopses: ~
    `angle` = |Body:getAngle|()

Variant: ~
    `angle` = |Body:getAngle|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `angle`
            The angle in radians.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Body:getAngularDamping* *love-Body:getAngularDamping*
|Body:getAngularDamping|

Gets the Angular damping of the Body

The angular damping is the ''rate of decrease of the angular velocity over
time'': A spinning body with no damping and no external forces will continue
spinning indefinitely. A spinning body with damping will gradually stop spinning.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together. However,
only damping is provided by Box2D (and LOVE).

Damping parameters should be between 0 and infinity, with 0 meaning no damping,
and infinity meaning full damping. Normally you will use a damping value between
0 and 0.1.

Synopses: ~
    `damping` = |Body:getAngularDamping|()

Variant: ~
    `damping` = |Body:getAngularDamping|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `damping`
            The value of the angular damping.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-Body:getAngularVelocity* *love-Body:getAngularVelocity*
|Body:getAngularVelocity|

Get the angular velocity of the Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

It is changed in World:update by applying torques, off centre forces/impulses,
and angular damping. It can be set directly with Body:setAngularVelocity.

If you need the ''rate of change of position over time'', use
Body:getLinearVelocity.

Synopses: ~
    `w` = |Body:getAngularVelocity|()

Variant: ~
    `w` = |Body:getAngularVelocity|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `w`
            The angular velocity in radians/second.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Synopses: ~
    `contacts` = |Body:getContacts|()

Variant: ~
    `contacts` = |Body:getContacts|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`table`) `contacts`
            A list with all contacts associated with the Body.

    See function description

        (`table`) `fixtures`
            A sequence with all fixtures.

    `inertia` = |Body:getInertia|()

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-Body:getLinearDamping* *love-Body:getLinearDamping*
|Body:getLinearDamping|

Gets the linear damping of the Body.

The linear damping is the ''rate of decrease of the linear velocity over time''.
A moving body with no damping and no external forces will continue moving
indefinitely, as is the case in space. A moving body with damping will gradually
stop moving.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together.

Synopses: ~
    `damping` = |Body:getLinearDamping|()

Variant: ~
    `damping` = |Body:getLinearDamping|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `damping`
            The value of the linear damping.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Body:getLinearVelocity* *love-Body:getLinearVelocity*
|Body:getLinearVelocity|

Gets the linear velocity of the Body from its center of mass.

The linear velocity is the ''rate of change of position over time''.

If you need the ''rate of change of angle over time'', use Body:getAngularVelocity.

If you need to get the linear velocity of a point different from the center of mass:

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint allows you to specify the point in local
coordinates.

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint allows you to specify the point in world
coordinates.

See page 136 of 'Essential Mathematics for Games and Interactive Applications'
for definitions of local and world coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    `x`, `y` = |Body:getLinearVelocity|()

Variant: ~
    `x`, `y` = |Body:getLinearVelocity|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x`
            The x-component of the velocity vector

        2. (`number`) `y`
            The y-component of the velocity vector

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint*
                                     *love-Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint*
|Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint|

Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center
of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in local coordinates. Use
Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint to specify this with world coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    `vx`, `vy` = |Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint|(`x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    `vx`, `vy` = |Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position to measure velocity.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position to measure velocity.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `vx`
            The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

        2. (`number`) `vy`
            The y component of velocity at point (x,y).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint*
                                     *love-Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint*
|Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint|

Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center
of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in world coordinates. Use
Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint to specify this with local coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    `vx`, `vy` = |Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint|(`x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    `vx`, `vy` = |Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position to measure velocity.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position to measure velocity.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `vx`
            The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

        2. (`number`) `vy`
            The y component of velocity at point (x,y).

    `localX`, `localY` = |Body:getLocalPoint|(`worldX`, `worldY`)

            The x position in world coordinates.
        • {worldY} (`number`)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Body:getLocalPoints* *love-Body:getLocalPoints*
|Body:getLocalPoints|

Transforms multiple points from world coordinates to local coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`, `...` = |Body:getLocalPoints|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`,
    `y2`, `...`)

Variant: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`, `...` = |Body:getLocalPoints|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`,
    `y2`, `...`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x1} (`number`)
            (Argument) The x position of the first point.
        • {y1} (`number`)
            (Argument) The y position of the first point.
        • {x2} (`number`)
            (Argument) The x position of the second point.
        • {y2} (`number`)
            (Argument) The y position of the second point.
        • {...} (`number`)
            (Argument) You can continue passing x and y position of the points.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x1`
            (Result) The transformed x position of the first point.

        2. (`number`) `y1`
            (Result) The transformed y position of the first point.

        3. (`number`) `x2`
            (Result) The transformed x position of the second point.

        4. (`number`) `y2`
            (Result) The transformed y position of the second point.

        5. (`number`) `...`
            (Result) Additional transformed x and y position of the points.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Body:getLocalVector* *love-Body:getLocalVector*
|Body:getLocalVector|

Transform a vector from world coordinates to local coordinates.

Synopses: ~
    `localX`, `localY` = |Body:getLocalVector|(`worldX`, `worldY`)

Variant: ~
    `localX`, `localY` = |Body:getLocalVector|(`worldX`, `worldY`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {worldX} (`number`)
            The vector x component in world coordinates.
        • {worldY} (`number`)
            The vector y component in world coordinates.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `localX`
            The vector x component in local coordinates.

        2. (`number`) `localY`
            The vector y component in local coordinates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Body:isBullet* *love-Body:isBullet*
|Body:isBullet|

Get the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes
jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a
bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when
moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not
let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

Synopses: ~
    `status` = |Body:isBullet|()

Variant: ~
    `status` = |Body:isBullet|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `status`
            The bullet status of the body.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-Body:isDestroyed* *love-Body:isDestroyed*
|Body:isDestroyed|

Gets whether the Body is destroyed. Destroyed bodies cannot be used.

Synopses: ~
    `destroyed` = |Body:isDestroyed|()

Variant: ~
    `destroyed` = |Body:isDestroyed|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `destroyed`
            Whether the Body is destroyed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    `fixed` = |Body:isFixedRotation|()

    `fixed` = |Body:isFixedRotation|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `fixed`
            True if the body's rotation is locked or false if not.

                             *LOVE-Body:resetMassData* *love-Body:resetMassData*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-Body:setActive* *love-Body:setActive*
|Body:setActive|

Sets whether the body is active in the world.

An inactive body does not take part in the simulation. It will not move or cause
any collisions.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setActive|(`active`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setActive|(`active`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {active} (`boolean`)
            If the body is active or not.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Body:setAngle* *love-Body:setAngle*
|Body:setAngle|

Set the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it from degrees, use
math.rad.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase
counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our
point of view.

It is possible to cause a collision with another body by changing its angle.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setAngle|(`angle`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setAngle|(`angle`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {angle} (`number`)
            The angle in radians.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Body:setAngularDamping* *love-Body:setAngularDamping*
|Body:setAngularDamping|

Sets the angular damping of a Body

See Body:getAngularDamping for a definition of angular damping.

Angular damping can take any value from 0 to infinity. It is recommended to stay
between 0 and 0.1, though. Other values will look unrealistic.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setAngularDamping|(`damping`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setAngularDamping|(`damping`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {damping} (`number`)
            The new angular damping.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-Body:setAngularVelocity* *love-Body:setAngularVelocity*
|Body:setAngularVelocity|

Sets the angular velocity of a Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

This function will not accumulate anything; any impulses previously applied
since the last call to World:update will be lost.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setAngularVelocity|(`w`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setAngularVelocity|(`w`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {w} (`number`)
            The new angular velocity, in radians per second

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Body:setAwake* *love-Body:setAwake*
|Body:setAwake|

Wakes the body up or puts it to sleep.

    |Body:setAwake|(`awake`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setAwake|(`awake`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {awake} (`boolean`)
            The body sleep status.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                     *LOVE-Body:setBullet* *love-Body:setBullet*
|Body:setBullet|

Set the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes
jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a
bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when
moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not
let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setBullet|(`status`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setBullet|(`status`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {status} (`boolean`)
            The bullet status of the body.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-Body:setFixedRotation* *love-Body:setFixedRotation*
|Body:setFixedRotation|

Set whether a body has fixed rotation.

Bodies with fixed rotation don't vary the speed at which they rotate. Calling
this function causes the mass to be reset.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setFixedRotation|(`isFixed`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setFixedRotation|(`isFixed`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {isFixed} (`boolean`)
            Whether the body should have fixed rotation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-Body:setGravityScale* *love-Body:setGravityScale*
|Body:setGravityScale|

Sets a new gravity scale factor for the body.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setGravityScale|(`scale`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setGravityScale|(`scale`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {scale} (`number`)
            The new gravity scale factor.

                                   *LOVE-Body:setInertia* *love-Body:setInertia*

    |Body:setInertia|(`inertia`)

    |Body:setInertia|(`inertia`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {inertia} (`number`)
            The new moment of inertia, in kilograms * pixel squared.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-Body:setLinearDamping* *love-Body:setLinearDamping*
|Body:setLinearDamping|

Sets the linear damping of a Body

See Body:getLinearDamping for a definition of linear damping.

Linear damping can take any value from 0 to infinity. It is recommended to stay
between 0 and 0.1, though. Other values will make the objects look 'floaty'(if
gravity is enabled).

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setLinearDamping|(`ld`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setLinearDamping|(`ld`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {ld} (`number`)
            The new linear damping

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    |Body:setMassData|(`x`, `y`, `mass`, `inertia`)

                                 *LOVE-Body:setPosition* *love-Body:setPosition*

This function cannot wake up the body.

    |Body:setPosition|(`x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setPosition|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-Body:setSleepingAllowed* *love-Body:setSleepingAllowed*
|Body:setSleepingAllowed|

Sets the sleeping behaviour of the body. Should sleeping be allowed, a body at
rest will automatically sleep. A sleeping body is not simulated unless it
collided with an awake body. Be wary that one can end up with a situation like a
floating sleeping body if the floor was removed.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setSleepingAllowed|(`allowed`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setSleepingAllowed|(`allowed`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {allowed} (`boolean`)
            True if the body is allowed to sleep or false if not.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                               *LOVE-Body:setTransform* *love-Body:setTransform*
|Body:setTransform|

Set the position and angle of the body.

Note that the position may not be the center of mass of the body. An angle of 0
radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase
counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes clockwise from our point
of view.

This function cannot wake up the body.

Synopses: ~
    |Body:setTransform|(`x`, `y`, `angle`)

Variant: ~
    |Body:setTransform|(`x`, `y`, `angle`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x component of the position.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y component of the position.
        • {angle} (`number`)
            The angle in radians.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                             *LOVE-ChainShape* *love-ChainShape*
|ChainShape|

A ChainShape consists of multiple line segments. It can be used to create the
boundaries of your terrain. The shape does not have volume and can only collide
with PolygonShape and CircleShape.

Unlike the PolygonShape, the ChainShape does not have a vertices limit or has to
form a convex shape, but self intersections are not supported.

                   *LOVE-ChainShape-constructors* *love-ChainShape-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        |newChainShape|                                     |LOVE-newChainShape|

                       *LOVE-ChainShape-supertypes* *love-ChainShape-supertypes*
    Supertypes: ~
        |Shape|                                                     |LOVE-Shape|
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|

                           *LOVE-ChainShape-subtypes* *love-ChainShape-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                         *LOVE-ChainShape-functions* *love-ChainShape-functions*
    Functions: ~
        |getChildEdge|                            |LOVE-ChainShape:getChildEdge|
        |getNextVertex|                          |LOVE-ChainShape:getNextVertex|
        |getPoint|                                    |LOVE-ChainShape:getPoint|
        |getPoints|                                  |LOVE-ChainShape:getPoints|
        |getPreviousVertex|                  |LOVE-ChainShape:getPreviousVertex|
        |getVertexCount|                        |LOVE-ChainShape:getVertexCount|
        |setNextVertex|                          |LOVE-ChainShape:setNextVertex|
        |setPreviousVertex|                  |LOVE-ChainShape:setPreviousVertex|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-ChainShape:getChildEdge* *love-ChainShape:getChildEdge*
|ChainShape:getChildEdge|

Returns a child of the shape as an EdgeShape.

Synopses: ~
    `shape` = |ChainShape:getChildEdge|(`index`)

Variant: ~
    `shape` = |ChainShape:getChildEdge|(`index`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {index} (`number`)
            The index of the child.

    Return: ~
        (`EdgeShape`) `shape` |LOVE-EdgeShape|
            The child as an EdgeShape.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-ChainShape:getNextVertex* *love-ChainShape:getNextVertex*
|ChainShape:getNextVertex|

Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the next shape.

Setting next and previous ChainShape vertices can help prevent unwanted
collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

Synopses: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getNextVertex|()

Variant: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getNextVertex|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x`
            The x-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setNextVertex
            hasn't been called.

        2. (`number`) `y`
            The y-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setNextVertex
            hasn't been called.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-ChainShape:getPoint* *love-ChainShape:getPoint*
|ChainShape:getPoint|

Returns a point of the shape.

Synopses: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getPoint|(`index`)

Variant: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getPoint|(`index`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {index} (`number`)
            The index of the point to return.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x`
            The x-coordinate of the point.

        2. (`number`) `y`
            The y-coordinate of the point.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-ChainShape:getPoints* *love-ChainShape:getPoints*
|ChainShape:getPoints|

Returns all points of the shape.

Synopses: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |ChainShape:getPoints|()

Variant: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |ChainShape:getPoints|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x1`
            The x-coordinate of the first point.

        2. (`number`) `y1`
            The y-coordinate of the first point.

        3. (`number`) `x2`
            The x-coordinate of the second point.

        4. (`number`) `y2`
            The y-coordinate of the second point.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         *LOVE-ChainShape:getPreviousVertex* *love-ChainShape:getPreviousVertex*
|ChainShape:getPreviousVertex|

Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the previous shape.

Setting next and previous ChainShape vertices can help prevent unwanted
collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

Synopses: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getPreviousVertex|()

Variant: ~
    `x`, `y` = |ChainShape:getPreviousVertex|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~

            ChainShape:setPreviousVertex hasn't been called.

                                                   *LOVE-Contact* *love-Contact*
|Contact|

Contacts are objects created to manage collisions in worlds.

                         *LOVE-Contact-constructors* *love-Contact-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        None

                             *LOVE-Contact-supertypes* *love-Contact-supertypes*
    Supertypes: ~
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|

                                 *LOVE-Contact-subtypes* *love-Contact-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                               *LOVE-Contact-functions* *love-Contact-functions*
    Functions: ~
        |getChildren|                                 |LOVE-Contact:getChildren|
        |getFixtures|                                 |LOVE-Contact:getFixtures|

        |isEnabled|                                     |LOVE-Contact:isEnabled|

        |resetRestitution|                       |LOVE-Contact:resetRestitution|

        |setFriction|                                 |LOVE-Contact:setFriction|
        |setRestitution|                           |LOVE-Contact:setRestitution|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Contact:getChildren* *love-Contact:getChildren*
|Contact:getChildren|

Gets the child indices of the shapes of the two colliding fixtures. For
ChainShapes, an index of 1 is the first edge in the chain.
Used together with Fixture:rayCast or ChainShape:getChildEdge.

Synopses: ~
    `indexA`, `indexB` = |Contact:getChildren|()

Variant: ~
    `indexA`, `indexB` = |Contact:getChildren|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `indexA`
            The child index of the first fixture's shape.

        2. (`number`) `indexB`
            The child index of the second fixture's shape.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Contact:getFixtures* *love-Contact:getFixtures*
|Contact:getFixtures|

Gets the two Fixtures that hold the shapes that are in contact.

Synopses: ~
    `fixtureA`, `fixtureB` = |Contact:getFixtures|()

Variant: ~
    `fixtureA`, `fixtureB` = |Contact:getFixtures|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`Fixture`) `fixtureA` |LOVE-Fixture|
            The first Fixture.

        2. (`Fixture`) `fixtureB` |LOVE-Fixture|
            The second Fixture.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Contact:getFriction* *love-Contact:getFriction*
|Contact:getFriction|

Get the friction between two shapes that are in contact.

Synopses: ~
    `friction` = |Contact:getFriction|()

Variant: ~
    `friction` = |Contact:getFriction|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `friction`
            The friction of the contact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                               *LOVE-Contact:getNormal* *love-Contact:getNormal*
|Contact:getNormal|

Get the normal vector between two shapes that are in contact.

This function returns the coordinates of a unit vector that points from the
first shape to the second.

Synopses: ~
    `nx`, `ny` = |Contact:getNormal|()

Variant: ~
    `nx`, `ny` = |Contact:getNormal|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `nx`
            The x component of the normal vector.

        2. (`number`) `ny`
            The y component of the normal vector.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-Contact:getPositions* *love-Contact:getPositions*
|Contact:getPositions|

Returns the contact points of the two colliding fixtures. There can be one or
two points.

Synopses: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |Contact:getPositions|()

Variant: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |Contact:getPositions|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x1`
            The x coordinate of the first contact point.

        2. (`number`) `y1`
            The y coordinate of the first contact point.

        3. (`number`) `x2`
            The x coordinate of the second contact point.

        4. (`number`) `y2`
            The y coordinate of the second contact point.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Contact:getRestitution* *love-Contact:getRestitution*
|Contact:getRestitution|

Get the restitution between two shapes that are in contact.

Synopses: ~
    `restitution` = |Contact:getRestitution|()

Variant: ~
    `restitution` = |Contact:getRestitution|()

    See function description

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-Fixture:rayCast* *love-Fixture:rayCast*
|Fixture:rayCast|

Casts a ray against the shape of the fixture and returns the surface normal
vector and the line position where the ray hit. If the ray missed the shape, nil
will be returned.

The ray starts on the first point of the input line and goes towards the second
point of the line. The fifth argument is the maximum distance the ray is going
to travel as a scale factor of the input line length.

The childIndex parameter is used to specify which child of a parent shape, such
as a ChainShape, will be ray casted. For ChainShapes, the index of 1 is the
first edge on the chain. Ray casting a parent shape will only test the child
specified so if you want to test every shape of the parent, you must loop
through all of its children.

The world position of the impact can be calculated by multiplying the line
vector with the third return value and adding it to the line starting point.

hitx, hity = x1 + (x2 - x1) * fraction, y1 + (y2 - y1) * fraction

Synopses: ~
    `xn`, `yn`, `fraction` = |Fixture:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`,
    `maxFraction`, `childIndex`)

Variant: ~
    `xn`, `yn`, `fraction` = |Fixture:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`,
    `maxFraction`, `childIndex`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x1} (`number`)
            The x position of the input line starting point.
        • {y1} (`number`)
            The y position of the input line starting point.
        • {x2} (`number`)
            The x position of the input line end point.
        • {y2} (`number`)
            The y position of the input line end point.
        • {maxFraction} (`number`)
            Ray length parameter.
        • {childIndex} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            The index of the child the ray gets cast against.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `xn`
            The x component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit
            the shape.

        2. (`number`) `yn`
            The y component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit
            the shape.

        3. (`number`) `fraction`
            The position on the input line where the intersection happened as a
            factor of the line length.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Fixture:setCategory* *love-Fixture:setCategory*
|Fixture:setCategory|

Sets the categories the fixture belongs to. There can be up to 16 categories
represented as a number from 1 to 16.

All fixture's default category is 1.

                             *LOVE-Fixture:setDensity* *love-Fixture:setDensity*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-Fixture:setFilterData* *love-Fixture:setFilterData*
|Fixture:setFilterData|

Sets the filter data of the fixture.

Groups, categories, and mask can be used to define the collision behaviour of
the fixture.

If two fixtures are in the same group they either always collide if the group is
positive, or never collide if it's negative. If the group is zero or they do not
match, then the contact filter checks if the fixtures select a category of the
other fixture with their masks. The fixtures do not collide if that's not the
case. If they do have each other's categories selected, the return value of the
custom contact filter will be used. They always collide if none was set.

There can be up to 16 categories. Categories and masks are encoded as the bits
of a 16-bit integer.

When created, prior to calling this function, all fixtures have category set to
1, mask set to 65535 (all categories) and group set to 0.

This function allows setting all filter data for a fixture at once. To set only
the categories, the mask or the group, you can use Fixture:setCategory,
Fixture:setMask or Fixture:setGroupIndex respectively.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setFilterData|(`categories`, `mask`, `group`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setFilterData|(`categories`, `mask`, `group`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {categories} (`number`)
            The categories as an integer from 0 to 65535.
        • {mask} (`number`)
            The mask as an integer from 0 to 65535.
        • {group} (`number`)
            The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Fixture:setFriction* *love-Fixture:setFriction*
|Fixture:setFriction|

Sets the friction of the fixture.

Friction determines how shapes react when they 'slide' along other shapes. Low
friction indicates a slippery surface, like ice, while high friction indicates a
rough surface, like concrete. Range: 0.0 - 1.0.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setFriction|(`friction`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setFriction|(`friction`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {friction} (`number`)
            The fixture friction.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-Fixture:setGroupIndex* *love-Fixture:setGroupIndex*
|Fixture:setGroupIndex|

Sets the group the fixture belongs to. Fixtures with the same group will always
collide if the group is positive or never collide if it's negative. The group
zero means no group.

The groups range from -32768 to 32767.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setGroupIndex|(`group`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setGroupIndex|(`group`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {group} (`number`)
            The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-Fixture:setMask* *love-Fixture:setMask*
|Fixture:setMask|

Sets the category mask of the fixture. There can be up to 16 categories
represented as a number from 1 to 16.

This fixture will '''NOT''' collide with the fixtures that are in the selected
categories if the other fixture also has a category of this fixture selected.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setMask|(`...`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setMask|(`...`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {...} (`number`)
            The masks.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-Fixture:setRestitution* *love-Fixture:setRestitution*
|Fixture:setRestitution|

Sets the restitution of the fixture.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setRestitution|(`restitution`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setRestitution|(`restitution`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {restitution} (`number`)
            The fixture restitution.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                               *LOVE-Fixture:setSensor* *love-Fixture:setSensor*
|Fixture:setSensor|

Sets whether the fixture should act as a sensor.

Sensors do not cause collision responses, but the begin-contact and end-contact
World callbacks will still be called for this fixture.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setSensor|(`sensor`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setSensor|(`sensor`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {sensor} (`boolean`)
            The sensor status.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-Fixture:setUserData* *love-Fixture:setUserData*
|Fixture:setUserData|

Associates a Lua value with the fixture.

To delete the reference, explicitly pass nil.

Synopses: ~
    |Fixture:setUserData|(`value`)

Variant: ~
    |Fixture:setUserData|(`value`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {value} (`any`)
            The Lua value to associate with the fixture.

|Fixture:testPoint|

    `isInside` = |Fixture:testPoint|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x position of the point.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y position of the point.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `isInside`
            True if the point is inside or false if it is outside.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Joint:destroy* *love-Joint:destroy*
|Joint:destroy|

Explicitly destroys the Joint. An error will occur if you attempt to use the
object after calling this function.

In 0.7.2, when you don't have time to wait for garbage collection, this function

may be used to free the object immediately.

Synopses: ~
    |Joint:destroy|()

Variant: ~
    |Joint:destroy|()

    See function description

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-Joint:getAnchors* *love-Joint:getAnchors*

    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |Joint:getAnchors|()

Variant: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |Joint:getAnchors|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `x1`
            The x-component of the anchor on Body 1.

        2. (`number`) `y1`
            The y-component of the anchor on Body 1.

        3. (`number`) `x2`
            The x-component of the anchor on Body 2.

        4. (`number`) `y2`
            The y-component of the anchor on Body 2.

                     *LOVE-Joint:getReactionForce* *love-Joint:getReactionForce*

    `x`, `y` = |Joint:getReactionForce|(`x`)

    See function description

        • {x} (`number`)
            How long the force applies. Usually the inverse time step or 1/dt.

        1. (`number`) `x`
            The x-component of the force.

        2. (`number`) `y`
            The y-component of the force.

    `torque` = |Joint:getReactionTorque|(`invdt`)

    Parameters: ~

            How long the force applies. Usually the inverse time step or 1/dt.

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `torque`
            The reaction torque on the second body.

    `angleoffset` = |MotorJoint:getAngularOffset|()

                     *LOVE-PolygonShape:getPoints* *love-PolygonShape:getPoints*
|PolygonShape:getPoints|

Get the local coordinates of the polygon's vertices.

This function has a variable number of return values. It can be used in a nested
fashion with love.graphics.polygon.

Synopses: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |PolygonShape:getPoints|()

Variant: ~
    `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` = |PolygonShape:getPoints|()

    See function description

        1. (`number`) `x1`
            The x-component of the first vertex.

        2. (`number`) `y1`
            The y-component of the first vertex.

        3. (`number`) `x2`
            The x-component of the second vertex.

        4. (`number`) `y2`
            The y-component of the second vertex.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-PulleyJoint:setConstant* *love-PulleyJoint:setConstant*
|PulleyJoint:setConstant|

Set the total length of the rope.

Setting a new length for the rope updates the maximum length values of the joint.

Synopses: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setConstant|(`length`)

Variant: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setConstant|(`length`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {length} (`number`)
            The new length of the rope in the joint.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
               *LOVE-PulleyJoint:setMaxLengths* *love-PulleyJoint:setMaxLengths*
|PulleyJoint:setMaxLengths|

Set the maximum lengths of the rope segments.

The physics module also imposes maximum values for the rope segments. If the
parameters exceed these values, the maximum values are set instead of the
requested values.

Synopses: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setMaxLengths|(`max1`, `max2`)

Variant: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setMaxLengths|(`max1`, `max2`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {max1} (`number`)
            The new maximum length of the first segment.
        • {max2} (`number`)
            The new maximum length of the second segment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                         *LOVE-PulleyJoint:setRatio* *love-PulleyJoint:setRatio*
|PulleyJoint:setRatio|

Set the pulley ratio.

Synopses: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setRatio|(`ratio`)

Variant: ~
    |PulleyJoint:setRatio|(`ratio`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {ratio} (`number`)
            The new pulley ratio of the joint.

                                               *LOVE-RopeJoint* *love-RopeJoint*
|RopeJoint|

The RopeJoint enforces a maximum distance between two points on two bodies. It
has no other effect.

                     *LOVE-RopeJoint-constructors* *love-RopeJoint-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        |newRopeJoint|                                       |LOVE-newRopeJoint|

                         *LOVE-RopeJoint-supertypes* *love-RopeJoint-supertypes*

        |Joint|                                                     |LOVE-Joint|
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|

                             *LOVE-RopeJoint-subtypes* *love-RopeJoint-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                           *LOVE-RopeJoint-functions* *love-RopeJoint-functions*
    Functions: ~
        |getMaxLength|                             |LOVE-RopeJoint:getMaxLength|
        |setMaxLength|                             |LOVE-RopeJoint:setMaxLength|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                       *LOVE-Shape* *love-Shape*
|Shape|

Shapes are solid 2d geometrical objects which handle the mass and collision of a
Body in love.physics.

Shapes are attached to a Body via a Fixture. The Shape object is copied when
this happens.

The Shape's position is relative to the position of the Body it has been
attached to.

                             *LOVE-Shape-constructors* *love-Shape-constructors*
    Constructors: ~
        None

                                 *LOVE-Shape-supertypes* *love-Shape-supertypes*
    Supertypes: ~
        |Object|                                                   |LOVE-Object|

                                     *LOVE-Shape-subtypes* *love-Shape-subtypes*
    Subtypes: ~
        None

                                   *LOVE-Shape-functions* *love-Shape-functions*
    Functions: ~
        |computeAABB|                                   |LOVE-Shape:computeAABB|
        |computeMass|                                   |LOVE-Shape:computeMass|
        |getChildCount|                               |LOVE-Shape:getChildCount|
        |getRadius|                                       |LOVE-Shape:getRadius|
        |getType|                                           |LOVE-Shape:getType|
        |rayCast|                                           |LOVE-Shape:rayCast|
        |testPoint|                                       |LOVE-Shape:testPoint|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                               *LOVE-Shape:computeAABB* *love-Shape:computeAABB*
|Shape:computeAABB|

Returns the points of the bounding box for the transformed shape.

Synopses: ~
    `topLeftX`, `topLeftY`, `bottomRightX`, `bottomRightY` =
    |Shape:computeAABB|(`tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `childIndex`)

Variant: ~
    `topLeftX`, `topLeftY`, `bottomRightX`, `bottomRightY` =
    |Shape:computeAABB|(`tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `childIndex`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {tx} (`number`)
            The translation of the shape on the x-axis.
        • {ty} (`number`)
            The translation of the shape on the y-axis.
        • {tr} (`number`)
            The shape rotation.
        • {childIndex} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            The index of the child to compute the bounding box of.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `topLeftX`
            The x position of the top-left point.

        2. (`number`) `topLeftY`
            The y position of the top-left point.

        3. (`number`) `bottomRightX`
            The x position of the bottom-right point.

        4. (`number`) `bottomRightY`
            The y position of the bottom-right point.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-Shape:rayCast* *love-Shape:rayCast*
|Shape:rayCast|

Casts a ray against the shape and returns the surface normal vector and the line
position where the ray hit. If the ray missed the shape, nil will be returned.
The Shape can be transformed to get it into the desired position.

The ray starts on the first point of the input line and goes towards the second
point of the line. The fourth argument is the maximum distance the ray is going
to travel as a scale factor of the input line length.

The childIndex parameter is used to specify which child of a parent shape, such
as a ChainShape, will be ray casted. For ChainShapes, the index of 1 is the
first edge on the chain. Ray casting a parent shape will only test the child
specified so if you want to test every shape of the parent, you must loop
through all of its children.

The world position of the impact can be calculated by multiplying the line
vector with the third return value and adding it to the line starting point.

hitx, hity = x1 + (x2 - x1) * fraction, y1 + (y2 - y1) * fraction

Synopses: ~
    `xn`, `yn`, `fraction` = |Shape:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`,
    `maxFraction`, `tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `childIndex`)

Variant: ~
    `xn`, `yn`, `fraction` = |Shape:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`,
    `maxFraction`, `tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `childIndex`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x1} (`number`)
            The x position of the input line starting point.
        • {y1} (`number`)
            The y position of the input line starting point.
        • {x2} (`number`)
            The x position of the input line end point.
        • {y2} (`number`)
            The y position of the input line end point.
        • {maxFraction} (`number`)
            Ray length parameter.
        • {tx} (`number`)
            The translation of the shape on the x-axis.
        • {ty} (`number`)
            The translation of the shape on the y-axis.
        • {tr} (`number`)
            The shape rotation.
        • {childIndex} (`number`) (default: `1`)
            The index of the child the ray gets cast against.

    Returns: ~
        1. (`number`) `xn`
            The x component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit
            the shape.

        2. (`number`) `yn`
            The y component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit
            the shape.

        3. (`number`) `fraction`
            The position on the input line where the intersection happened as a
            factor of the line length.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                   *LOVE-Shape:testPoint* *love-Shape:testPoint*
|Shape:testPoint|

This is particularly useful for mouse interaction with the shapes. By looping
through all shapes and testing the mouse position with this function, we can
find which shapes the mouse touches.

Synopses: ~
    `hit` = |Shape:testPoint|(`tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    `hit` = |Shape:testPoint|(`tx`, `ty`, `tr`, `x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {tx} (`number`)
            Translates the shape along the x-axis.
        • {ty} (`number`)
            Translates the shape along the y-axis.
        • {tr} (`number`)
            Rotates the shape.
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x-component of the point.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y-component of the point.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `hit`
            True if inside, false if outside

            Gets called before a collision gets resolved.

        4. (`function`) `postSolve`
            Gets called after the collision has been resolved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-World:queryBoundingBox* *love-World:queryBoundingBox*
|World:queryBoundingBox|

Calls a function for each fixture inside the specified area by searching for any
overlapping bounding box (Fixture:getBoundingBox).

Synopses: ~
    |World:queryBoundingBox|(`topLeftX`, `topLeftY`, `bottomRightX`,
    `bottomRightY`, `callback`)

Variant: ~
    |World:queryBoundingBox|(`topLeftX`, `topLeftY`, `bottomRightX`,
    `bottomRightY`, `callback`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {topLeftX} (`number`)
            The x position of the top-left point.
        • {topLeftY} (`number`)
            The y position of the top-left point.
        • {bottomRightX} (`number`)
            The x position of the bottom-right point.
        • {bottomRightY} (`number`)
            The y position of the bottom-right point.
        • {callback} (`function`)
            This function gets passed one argument, the fixture, and should
            return a boolean. The search will continue if it is true or stop if
            it is false.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       *LOVE-World:rayCast* *love-World:rayCast*
|World:rayCast|

Casts a ray and calls a function for each fixtures it intersects.

Synopses: ~
    |World:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`, `callback`)

Variant: ~
    |World:rayCast|(`x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`, `callback`)

    There is a bug in LÖVE 0.8.0 where the normal vector passed to the callback
    function gets scaled by love.physics.getMeter.

    Parameters: ~
        • {x1} (`number`)
            The x position of the starting point of the ray.
        • {y1} (`number`)
            The x position of the starting point of the ray.
        • {x2} (`number`)
            The x position of the end point of the ray.
        • {y2} (`number`)
            The x value of the surface normal vector of the shape edge.
        • {callback} (`function`)
            A function called for each fixture intersected by the ray. The
            function gets six arguments and should return a number as a control
            value. The intersection points fed into the function will be in an
            arbitrary order. If you wish to find the closest point of
            intersection, you'll need to do that yourself within the function.
            The easiest way to do that is by using the fraction value.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             *LOVE-World:setCallbacks* *love-World:setCallbacks*
|World:setCallbacks|

Sets functions for the collision callbacks during the world update.

Four Lua functions can be given as arguments. The value nil removes a function.

When called, each function will be passed three arguments. The first two
arguments are the colliding fixtures and the third argument is the Contact
between them. The postSolve callback additionally gets the normal and tangent
impulse for each contact point. See notes.

If you are interested to know when exactly each callback is called, consult a
Box2d manual

Synopses: ~
    |World:setCallbacks|(`beginContact`, `endContact`, `preSolve`, `postSolve`)

Variant: ~
    |World:setCallbacks|(`beginContact`, `endContact`, `preSolve`, `postSolve`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {beginContact} (`function`)
            Gets called when two fixtures begin to overlap.
        • {endContact} (`function`)
            Gets called when two fixtures cease to overlap. This will also be
            called outside of a world update, when colliding objects are destroyed.
        • {preSolve} (`function`) (default: `nil`)
            Gets called before a collision gets resolved.
        • {postSolve} (`function`) (default: `nil`)
            Gets called after the collision has been resolved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     *LOVE-World:setContactFilter* *love-World:setContactFilter*
|World:setContactFilter|

Sets a function for collision filtering.

If the group and category filtering doesn't generate a collision decision, this
function gets called with the two fixtures as arguments. The function should
return a boolean value where true means the fixtures will collide and false
means they will pass through each other.

Synopses: ~
    |World:setContactFilter|(`filter`)

Variant: ~
    |World:setContactFilter|(`filter`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {filter} (`function`)
            The function handling the contact filtering.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                 *LOVE-World:setGravity* *love-World:setGravity*
|World:setGravity|

Set the gravity of the world.

Synopses: ~
    |World:setGravity|(`x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    |World:setGravity|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x component of gravity.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y component of gravity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-World:setSleepingAllowed* *love-World:setSleepingAllowed*
|World:setSleepingAllowed|

Sets the sleep behaviour of the world.

Synopses: ~
    |World:setSleepingAllowed|(`allow`)

Variant: ~
    |World:setSleepingAllowed|(`allow`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {allow} (`boolean`)
            True if bodies in the world are allowed to sleep, or false if not.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-World:translateOrigin* *love-World:translateOrigin*
|World:translateOrigin|

Translates the World's origin. Useful in large worlds where floating point
precision issues become noticeable at far distances from the origin.

Synopses: ~
    |World:translateOrigin|(`x`, `y`)

Variant: ~
    |World:translateOrigin|(`x`, `y`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {x} (`number`)
            The x component of the new origin with respect to the old origin.
        • {y} (`number`)
            The y component of the new origin with respect to the old origin.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                         *LOVE-World:update* *love-World:update*
|World:update|

Update the state of the world.

Synopses: ~
    |World:update|(`dt`, `velocityiterations`, `positioniterations`)

Variant: ~
    |World:update|(`dt`, `velocityiterations`, `positioniterations`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {dt} (`number`)
            The time (in seconds) to advance the physics simulation.
        • {velocityiterations} (`number`) (default: `8`)
            The maximum number of steps used to determine the new velocities
            when resolving a collision.
        • {positioniterations} (`number`) (default: `3`)
            The maximum number of steps used to determine the new positions when
            resolving a collision.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *LOVE-love.sound.newDecoder* *love-love.sound.newDecoder*
|love.sound.newDecoder|

Attempts to find a decoder for the encoded sound data in the specified file.

Synopses: ~
    1. `decoder` = |love.sound.newDecoder|(`file`, `buffer`)
    2. `decoder` = |love.sound.newDecoder|(`filename`, `buffer`)

Variants: ~
    1. `decoder` = |love.sound.newDecoder|(`file`, `buffer`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {file} (`File`) |LOVE-File|
            The file with encoded sound data.
        • {buffer} (`number`) (default: `2048`)
            The size of each decoded chunk, in bytes.

    Return: ~
        (`Decoder`) `decoder` |LOVE-Decoder|
            A new Decoder object.

    2. `decoder` = |love.sound.newDecoder|(`filename`, `buffer`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {filename} (`string`)
            The filename of the file with encoded sound data.
        • {buffer} (`number`) (default: `2048`)
            The size of each decoded chunk, in bytes.

    Return: ~
        (`Decoder`) `decoder` |LOVE-Decoder|
            A new Decoder object.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                   *LOVE-love.sound.newSoundData* *love-love.sound.newSoundData*
|love.sound.newSoundData|

Creates new SoundData from a filepath, File, or Decoder. It's also possible to
create SoundData with a custom sample rate, channel and bit depth.

The sound data will be decoded to the memory in a raw format. It is recommended
to create only short sounds like effects, as a 3 minute song uses 30 MB of
memory this way.

Synopses: ~
    1. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`filename`)
    2. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`file`)
    3. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`decoder`)
    4. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`samples`, `rate`, `bits`,
    `channels`)

Variants: ~
    1. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`filename`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {filename} (`string`)
            The file name of the file to load.

    Return: ~
        (`SoundData`) `soundData` |LOVE-SoundData|
            A new SoundData object.

    2. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`file`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {file} (`File`) |LOVE-File|
            A File pointing to an audio file.

    Return: ~
        (`SoundData`) `soundData` |LOVE-SoundData|
            A new SoundData object.

    3. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`decoder`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {decoder} (`Decoder`) |LOVE-Decoder|
            Decode data from this Decoder until EOF.

    Return: ~
        (`SoundData`) `soundData` |LOVE-SoundData|
            A new SoundData object.

    4. `soundData` = |love.sound.newSoundData|(`samples`, `rate`, `bits`,
    `channels`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {samples} (`number`)
            Total number of samples.
        • {rate} (`number`) (default: `44100`)
            Number of samples per second
        • {bits} (`number`) (default: `16`)
            Bits per sample (8 or 16).
        • {channels} (`number`) (default: `2`)
            Either 1 for mono or 2 for stereo.

    Return: ~
        (`SoundData`) `soundData` |LOVE-SoundData|
            A new SoundData object.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           *LOVE-SoundData:getSample* *love-SoundData:getSample*
|SoundData:getSample|

Gets the value of the sample-point at the specified position. For stereo
SoundData objects, the data from the left and right channels are interleaved in
that order.

Synopses: ~
    1. `sample` = |SoundData:getSample|(`i`)
    2. `sample` = |SoundData:getSample|(`i`, `channel`)

Variants: ~
    1. `sample` = |SoundData:getSample|(`i`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {i} (`number`)
            An integer value specifying the position of the sample (starting at 0).

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `sample`
            The normalized samplepoint (range -1.0 to 1.0).

    2. `sample` = |SoundData:getSample|(`i`, `channel`)

    Gets the value of a sample using an explicit sample index instead of
    interleaving them in the sample position parameter.

    Parameters: ~
        • {i} (`number`)
            An integer value specifying the position of the sample (starting at 0).
        • {channel} (`number`)
            The index of the channel to get within the given sample.

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `sample`
            The normalized samplepoint (range -1.0 to 1.0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-SoundData:getSampleCount* *love-SoundData:getSampleCount*
|SoundData:getSampleCount|

Synopses: ~
    `count` = |SoundData:getSampleCount|()

Variant: ~
    `count` = |SoundData:getSampleCount|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `count`
            Total number of samples.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                             *LOVE-PowerState* *love-PowerState*
|PowerState|

The basic state of the system's power supply.

    Constants: ~

        • {"unknown"} (`string`)
            Cannot determine power status.

        • {"battery"} (`string`)
            Not plugged in, running on a battery.

        • {"nobattery"} (`string`)
            Plugged in, no battery available.

        • {"charging"} (`string`)
            Plugged in, charging battery.

        • {"charged"} (`string`)
            Plugged in, battery is fully charged.

                               *LOVE-love.system.getOS* *love-love.system.getOS*
|love.system.getOS|

Gets the current operating system. In general, LÖVE abstracts away the need to
know the current operating system, but there are a few cases where it can be
useful (especially in combination with os.execute.)

Synopses: ~
    `osString` = |love.system.getOS|()

Variant: ~
    `osString` = |love.system.getOS|()

    In LÖVE version 0.8.0, the '''love._os''' string contains the current
    operating system.

    Return: ~
        (`string`) `osString`
            The current operating system. 'OS X', 'Windows', 'Linux', 'Android'
            or 'iOS'.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 *LOVE-love.system.getPowerInfo* *love-love.system.getPowerInfo*
|love.system.getPowerInfo|

Gets information about the system's power supply.

Variant: ~
    `state`, `percent`, `seconds` = |love.system.getPowerInfo|()

    See function description

    Returns: ~
        1. (`PowerState`) `state` |LOVE-PowerState|
            The basic state of the power supply.

        2. (`number`) `percent`
            Percentage of battery life left, between 0 and 100. nil if the value
            can't be determined or there's no battery.

        3. (`number`) `seconds`
            Seconds of battery life left. nil if the value can't be determined
            or there's no battery.

       *LOVE-love.system.getProcessorCount* *love-love.system.getProcessorCount*

    `processorCount` = |love.system.getProcessorCount|()

Variant: ~
    `processorCount` = |love.system.getProcessorCount|()

    The number includes the threads reported if technologies such as Intel's
    Hyper-threading are enabled. For example, on a 4-core CPU with
    Hyper-threading, this function will return 8.

    Return: ~
        (`number`) `processorCount`
            Amount of logical processors.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     *LOVE-love.system.hasBackgroundMusic* *love-love.system.hasBackgroundMusic*
|love.system.hasBackgroundMusic|

Gets whether another application on the system is playing music in the background.

Currently this is implemented on iOS and Android, and will always return false
on other operating systems. The t.audio.mixwithsystem flag in love.conf can be
used to configure whether background audio / music from other apps should play
while LÖVE is open.

Synopses: ~
    `backgroundmusic` = |love.system.hasBackgroundMusic|()

Variant: ~
    `backgroundmusic` = |love.system.hasBackgroundMusic|()

    See function description

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `backgroundmusic`
            True if the user is playing music in the background via another app,
            false otherwise.

    `success` = |love.system.openURL|(`url`)

Variant: ~
    `success` = |love.system.openURL|(`url`)

    Passing file:// scheme in Android 7.0 (Nougat) and later always result in
    failure. Prior to 11.2, this will crash LÖVE instead of returning false.

    Parameters: ~
        • {url} (`string`)
            The URL to open. Must be formatted as a proper URL.

    Return: ~
        (`boolean`) `success`
            Whether the URL was opened successfully.

                           *LOVE-love.system.vibrate* *love-love.system.vibrate*
|love.system.vibrate|

Causes the device to vibrate, if possible. Currently this will only work on
Android and iOS devices that have a built-in vibration motor.

Synopses: ~
    |love.system.vibrate|(`seconds`)

Variant: ~
    |love.system.vibrate|(`seconds`)

    See function description

    Parameters: ~
        • {seconds} (`number`) (default: `0.5`)
            The duration to vibrate for. If called on an iOS device, it will
            always vibrate for 0.5 seconds due to limitations in the iOS system
            APIs.
